content.text: Streaming UI components render AI output incrementally as tokens arrive, rather than waiting for the model to finish. The key challenges are: (1) managing a buffer of partial text, (2) parsing incomplete JSON for structured components, (3) scroll behaviour during live output, and (4) aborting mid-stream when the user clicks Stop. This topic covers each pattern as a self-contained React hook or component.

attrs.alt: Streaming UI state machine: idle → streaming_text (tokens) / streaming_tool (partial JSON) → complete or error; abort returns to idle from any state

content.text: The most common pattern: open a fetch stream to the AG-UI endpoint, parse SSE lines as they arrive, and append each
AbortController is stored in a ref (not state) to avoid stale closures in the cleanup function
AbortError is not an application error — catch it separately and reset to idle rather than error state
The cursor span uses animate-pulse (Tailwind) — replace with a CSS keyframe if Tailwind is unavailable
Decoding with stream: true prevents splitting multi-byte UTF-8 characters across chunk boundaries
Tool call arguments stream as partial JSON. The technique: accumulate the raw string in a ref, attempt
on every render, and show a skeleton until the parse succeeds. Once the full JSON is valid, swap the skeleton for the real component.
Never pass a type assertion on the parsed result — the model can produce unexpected shapes even with a schema
The isStreaming prop dims the card (opacity-70) until the stream closes, signalling the data may be incomplete
Skeleton divs use the same dimensions as the real card to prevent layout shift on swap
For deeply nested structures, parse incrementally with a streaming JSON parser (e.g. oboe.js) to update sub-fields before the whole object is valid
Wrapping the fetch in an AbortController lets you cancel mid-stream. The
function that aborts the current request and resets the running state. Starting a new request automatically aborts any previous one.

A message list should scroll to the bottom as new tokens arrive, but stop auto-scrolling when the user manually scrolls up. Use an IntersectionObserver or a simple scroll-position check to detect user intent.

Syntax highlighting on partial code causes flickering — the highlighter sees incomplete tokens and misidentifies them. The safe strategy: display the code as plain monospace during streaming and re-highlight after each render. Libraries like Prism are synchronous, so calling
The Anthropic API can emit 20–40 tokens per second. Calling

on every token triggers a re-render at that rate, which is fine for short responses but can cause jank on long outputs — especially when each render also syntax-highlights code. Debouncing at 16ms (one animation frame) batches tokens into ~60fps updates:
Stream URL → display when Content-Length received
with pre-written SSE events. This tests the full parse-and-render path without a real server.

Streaming UI components have four responsibilities: consuming the token stream efficiently, rendering incomplete text without flicker, parsing partial JSON for structured components, and cancelling gracefully when the user stops generation. The core building blocks are: an
ref per request, a text buffer in
fed by the SSE reader, a
attempt on every tool args update (with a skeleton fallback), and a scroll sentinel at the bottom of the message list. Debounce renders at 16ms for long outputs and always clean up the stream in the
return function to prevent memory leaks on unmount.
